title: Minimum Window Subsequence
description: Find shortest substring containing another string as subsequence

examples.explanation: 1. Initialize shortest = None, min_len = infinity. 2. Outer loop i=0: substring "a" -> check subsequence: T="bde" not in "a" -> invalid. i=0, j=1: "ab" -> check: match b at index 1, then d not found -> invalid. Continue until i=0, j=4: "abcde" -> check: b at index 1, d at index 3, e at index 4 -> valid, length=5, update shortest="abcde", min_len=5. i=0, j=5: "abcdeb" -> check: valid (b at 1, d at 3, e at 4), length=6, not shorter. i=0, j=6: "abcdebd" -> valid, length=7. i=0, j=7: "abcdebdd" -> valid, length=8. i=0, j=8: "abcdebdde" -> valid, length=9. 3. i=1: substring "b" -> invalid (needs d,e). i=1, j=2: "bc" -> invalid. i=1, j=3: "bcd" -> invalid (needs e). i=1, j=4: "bcde" -> check: b at index 1, d at index 3, e at index 4 -> valid, length=4, update shortest="bcde", min_len=4. Continue all i,j: no shorter found. 4. Return "bcde".

1. shortest = None, min_len = inf. 2. Outer loop i=0: substring "j" -> invalid. i=1: "m" -> check: T="m" matches at index 1 -> valid, length=1, update shortest="m", min_len=1. 3. Continue checking other substrings: any substring containing "m" will have length >=1, so "m" remains shortest. 4. Return "m".

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^2 * m)
approaches.summary: Generate all substrings of the main string, check if each contains the target as a subsequence, and track the shortest valid substring. Use a helper function to verify subsequence inclusion by iterating through characters.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Window Subsequence via Brute Force
ladders.desc: Find the shortest substring of S that contains T as a subsequence by checking all possible substrings.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the Minimum Window Subsequence problem by systematically examining every possible substring of the main string S to find the shortest one that contains the target string T as a subsequence. The intuition is straightforward: if we check all substrings, we're guaranteed to find the optimal solution, though this comes at a high computational cost. The algorithm works by generating all substrings of S using two nested loops: the outer loop fixes the starting index i, and the inner loop extends the ending index j from i to the end of S. For each substring S[i:j+1], we use a helper function to verify if T is a subsequence of it. This helper function iterates through both strings with two pointers, advancing the pointer in T only when a matching character is found in the substring. If we successfully traverse all characters of T, the substring is valid, and we update the shortest length found so far. The time complexity is O(n² * m), where n is the length of S and m is the length of T, because there are O(n²) substrings and checking each takes O(m) time. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables for tracking. This approach connects directly to the problem by implementing the complete solution, but it's inefficient for large inputs. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in substring indices, not handling empty strings correctly, and forgetting to update the shortest substring when a tie occurs (though the problem typically expects any valid shortest substring). The real code pattern involves nested loops and a subsequence check helper.

examples.trace: 1. Initialize shortest = None, min_len = infinity. 2. Outer loop i=0: substring "a" -> check subsequence: T="bde" not in "a" -> invalid. i=0, j=1: "ab" -> check: match b at index 1, then d not found -> invalid. Continue until i=0, j=4: "abcde" -> check: b at index 1, d at index 3, e at index 4 -> valid, length=5, update shortest="abcde", min_len=5. i=0, j=5: "abcdeb" -> check: valid (b at 1, d at 3, e at 4), length=6, not shorter. i=0, j=6: "abcdebd" -> valid, length=7. i=0, j=7: "abcdebdd" -> valid, length=8. i=0, j=8: "abcdebdde" -> valid, length=9. 3. i=1: substring "b" -> invalid (needs d,e). i=1, j=2: "bc" -> invalid. i=1, j=3: "bcd" -> invalid (needs e). i=1, j=4: "bcde" -> check: b at index 1, d at index 3, e at index 4 -> valid, length=4, update shortest="bcde", min_len=4. Continue all i,j: no shorter found. 4. Return "bcde".

1. shortest = None, min_len = inf. 2. Outer loop i=0: substring "j" -> invalid. i=1: "m" -> check: T="m" matches at index 1 -> valid, length=1, update shortest="m", min_len=1. 3. Continue checking other substrings: any substring containing "m" will have length >=1, so "m" remains shortest. 4. Return "m".

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Subsequence Check for Two Strings
ladders.desc: Determine if string T is a subsequence of string S by iterating through both strings with pointers.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core helper function used in the brute force approach to check if one string (T) is a subsequence of another string (S). The intuition is that a subsequence means all characters of T must appear in S in the same order, but not necessarily consecutively. We can verify this by scanning S from left to right and matching characters of T in order. The algorithm uses two pointers: i for S and j for T. Start with i=0 and j=0. While i < len(S) and j < len(T), if S[i] equals T[j], increment j to move to the next character in T. Always increment i to move through S. After the loop, if j equals len(T), it means all characters of T were found in order in S, so T is a subsequence of S; otherwise, it is not. The time complexity is O(n + m) in the worst case, where n is the length of S and m is the length of T, as we traverse each string once. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the level above (L0) by being the verification step for each substring. Common mistakes include not resetting pointers for each check, incorrect loop conditions, and off-by-one errors. The real code pattern is a while loop with pointer updates.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generating All Substrings of a String
ladders.desc: Enumerate all possible contiguous substrings of a given string using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves generating every possible substring of a string S, which is essential for the brute force approach to explore all candidate windows. The intuition is that a substring is defined by a start index i and an end index j, where i ≤ j, and includes all characters from i to j inclusive. By varying i and j over all valid indices, we can cover all substrings. The algorithm uses two nested loops: the outer loop iterates i from 0 to len(S)-1, and for each i, the inner loop iterates j from i to len(S)-1. For each pair (i, j), we extract the substring S[i:j+1] (using Python slicing notation). This generates all substrings in order of increasing start index and length. The time complexity is O(n²) for generating substrings, as there are n*(n+1)/2 substrings. Space complexity is O(1) if we just process each substring without storing all, but extracting each substring can take O(n) space temporarily. This connects to L1 by providing the substrings to check for subsequence inclusion. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, misunderstanding substring inclusivity, and inefficiency from storing all substrings. The real code pattern is nested for-loops with slicing.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Slicing and Indexing
ladders.desc: Extract portions of a string using start and end indices in programming.
ladders.explanation: String slicing is a fundamental operation that allows extracting a substring from a larger string by specifying a start index and an end index. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can access contiguous segments using indices. In most programming languages, string slicing is done with syntax like S[start:end], which returns characters from index start inclusive to end exclusive. For example, in Python, S[1:4] gives characters at indices 1, 2, and 3. If end is omitted, it goes to the end of the string; if start is omitted, it starts from the beginning. Negative indices can count from the end. The algorithm involves specifying the indices and using built-in functions or loops to copy the characters. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the slice, as it may need to copy characters. Space complexity is O(k) for the new substring. This connects to L2 by being the method to generate each substring. Common mistakes include confusion between inclusive/exclusive bounds, off-by-one errors, and assuming slicing modifies the original string (it doesn't). The real code pattern uses square brackets with colon.

examples.trace: 1. S = "hello", indices: 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'. 2. Slice S[1:4] takes characters from index 1 to 3 inclusive: 'e', 'l', 'l'. 3. Result: "ell".
1. S = "world", indices: 0='w', 1='o', 2='r', 3='l', 4='d'. 2. Slice S[0:2] takes indices 0 and 1: 'w', 'o'. 3. Result: "wo".

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through a String with Index
ladders.desc: Loop through each character of a string while tracking its position using an index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing a string character by character, typically using a loop that increments an index variable. The intuition is that strings are ordered sequences, and we often need to access each element in order, possibly to perform checks or modifications. The algorithm uses a for-loop or while-loop with an index i starting at 0 and going up to len(string)-1. In each iteration, we access string[i] to get the character at that position. For example, in Python, for i in range(len(S)): char = S[i]. This allows us to examine or process each character based on its index. Time complexity is O(n) for a string of length n, as we visit each character once. Space complexity is O(1) if we don't store extra data. This connects to L3 by being how we implement slicing or other string manipulations. Common mistakes include going out of bounds (e.g., accessing index equal to length), using the wrong loop type, or not updating the index correctly. The real code pattern is a for-loop with range.

examples.trace: 1. Initialize i=0. 2. i=0: S[0]='c'. 3. i=1: S[1]='a'. 4. i=2: S[2]='t'. 5. i=3: stop as i=3 equals len(S)=3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a String?
ladders.desc: Understand strings as sequences of characters in programming.
ladders.explanation: A string is a fundamental data type in programming that represents a sequence of characters. Characters can include letters, digits, symbols, or spaces. The intuition is that strings are used to store and manipulate text. In most languages, strings are immutable, meaning once created, they cannot be changed; operations on strings create new strings. Strings have properties like length (number of characters) and support operations such as concatenation (joining two strings), accessing individual characters by index (starting from 0), and slicing (extracting parts). For example, the string "hello" has length 5, with 'h' at index 0, 'e' at index 1, etc. This concept is foundational because it underpins all string manipulation tasks. Time and space considerations depend on operations; e.g., accessing a character is O(1), but creating a new string may be O(n). This connects to L4 as the basis for iteration. Common mistakes include confusing strings with lists, forgetting that indices start at 0, and not handling empty strings. The real code pattern involves defining strings with quotes.

examples.trace: 1. Define string S = "apple". 2. Length is number of characters: 5. 3. Characters: index 0='a', 1='p', 2='p', 3='l', 4='e'.
1. S = "42". 2. Length: 2. 3. Characters: index 0='4', 1='2'.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Next Array O(n * m)
approaches.summary: Precompute a next array to store, for each position in the main string and each character, the next occurrence index. Then, for each starting index, use this array to greedily find the shortest window containing the target subsequence.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Window Subsequence with Next Array DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dynamic programming with next array to find shortest substring containing target as subsequence.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to precompute for every position in the main string and every possible character, the next occurrence index. This allows us to efficiently jump through the main string when matching the target subsequence. The algorithm works by first building a next array where next[i][c] stores the smallest index j >= i where s[j] equals character c, or -1 if no such character exists. Then, for each starting position i in the main string, we use this next array to greedily find the shortest window that contains the target as a subsequence. We iterate through each character in the target, using next to jump to the next matching position in the main string. If at any point we can't find a match (next returns -1), we break. Otherwise, we record the window from starting position to the final matched position. We track the minimum window across all starting positions. The time complexity is O(n * m) where n is length of main string and m is length of target, because building the next array takes O(n * 26) and checking each starting position takes O(m) using the precomputed next array. Space complexity is O(n * 26) for the next array. This approach is efficient because it avoids repeatedly scanning the string for character matches. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling the case where no window exists, 2) Off-by-one errors in next array construction, 3) Forgetting to update the starting position correctly when searching for windows. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the next array construction as its key subroutine.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build next array for s = "abcdebdde"
For position 0 (a): next[0]['a']=0, next[0]['b']=1, next[0]['c']=2, next[0]['d']=3, next[0]['e']=4, others=-1
For position 1 (b): next[1]['b']=1, next[1]['c']=2, next[1]['d']=3, next[1]['e']=4, others=-1
... continue for all positions
Step 2: Try starting at i=0
Match t[0]='b': next[0]['b']=1
Match t[1]='d': next[1]['d']=3
Match t[2]='e': next[3]['e']=4
Window from 0 to 4 = "abcde" (length 5)
Step 3: Try starting at i=1
Match t[0]='b': next[1]['b']=1
Match t[1]='d': next[1]['d']=3
Match t[2]='e': next[3]['e']=4
Window from 1 to 4 = "bcde" (length 4)
Step 4: Try starting at i=2
Match t[0]='b': next[2]['b']=5 (since b at position 5)
Match t[1]='d': next[5]['d']=6
Match t[2]='e': next[6]['e']=7
Window from 2 to 7 = "cdebdde" (length 6)
... continue all starting positions
Minimum window is "bcde" from position 1 to 4.

Step 1: Build next array for s
Step 2: Since t has only one character 'm', we find the shortest window containing 'm'
Step 3: Starting at i=0: next[0]['m']=1 (m at position 1)
Window from 0 to 1 = "jm" (length 2)
Step 4: Starting at i=1: next[1]['m']=1
Window from 1 to 1 = "m" (length 1)
Step 5: Minimum window is "m" at position 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Building the Next Array
ladders.desc: Construct next array that stores for each position and character, the next occurrence index.
ladders.explanation: The intuition for building the next array is to create a lookup table that allows O(1) access to find where a given character appears next from any position in the string. This is crucial for efficiently matching subsequences without repeatedly scanning the string. The algorithm works backward from the end of the string to the beginning. We maintain a current array that tracks the next occurrence of each character from the current position. Starting from the last character, we set next[i][c] = i if s[i] equals c, otherwise we copy from next[i+1][c]. This backward propagation ensures that each position knows about all future occurrences. The time complexity is O(n * 26) where n is string length and 26 is alphabet size (assuming lowercase English letters). Space complexity is O(n * 26). Common mistakes include: 1) Building forward instead of backward (which doesn't give next occurrence), 2) Not initializing the last row correctly, 3) Forgetting to handle characters not in the alphabet. The connection to L0 is that this next array is the data structure that enables the efficient window finding in the full solution.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize next as 2D array of size 3×26 filled with -1
Step 2: Process position 2 (last character 'c')
next[2]['c'] = 2 (since s[2]='c')
All other characters remain -1
Step 3: Process position 1 (character 'b')
Copy next[2] to next[1] for all characters
Then set next[1]['b'] = 1 (since s[1]='b')
Now next[1]['b']=1, next[1]['c']=2, others=-1
Step 4: Process position 0 (character 'a')
Copy next[1] to next[0] for all characters
Then set next[0]['a'] = 0
Final next array:
Position 0: a=0, b=1, c=2, others=-1
Position 1: b=1, c=2, others=-1
Position 2: c=2, others=-1

Step 1: Initialize next as 3×26 filled with -1
Step 2: Position 2 (character 'a')
next[2]['a'] = 2
Step 3: Position 1 (character 'b')
Copy next[2] to next[1]
Set next[1]['b'] = 1
Now next[1]['a']=2, next[1]['b']=1
Step 4: Position 0 (character 'a')
Copy next[1] to next[0]
Set next[0]['a'] = 0
Final next array:
Position 0: a=0, b=1, others=-1
Position 1: a=2, b=1, others=-1
Position 2: a=2, others=-1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Subsequence Matching with Next Array
ladders.desc: Using precomputed next array to efficiently find if a subsequence exists from a starting position.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that with a next array, we can match a target subsequence in O(m) time from any starting position, where m is the target length. The intuition is that instead of scanning through the main string looking for each character, we can use the next array to immediately jump to the next occurrence of the needed character. The algorithm starts at a given position i in the main string. For each character in the target, we look up next[i][current_char]. If it's -1, the subsequence doesn't exist from this starting position. Otherwise, we update i to next[i][current_char] + 1 to continue searching for the next character. The +1 is crucial because we need to look after the matched character for the next target character. This greedy approach works because the next array gives us the earliest possible match for each character, which minimizes the window size. Time complexity is O(m) per starting position. Space complexity is O(1) beyond the precomputed next array. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to add 1 when advancing i, 2) Not handling the -1 case properly, 3) Confusing indices between 0-based and 1-based. The connection to L1 is that this logic uses the next array built in that level to perform efficient matching.

examples.input: s = "abcde", next array from L1, start=0, t = "ace"
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at i=0, match t[0]='a'
next[0]['a'] = 0 (found at position 0)
Update i = 0 + 1 = 1
Step 2: Match t[1]='c'
next[1]['c'] = 2 (found at position 2)
Update i = 2 + 1 = 3
Step 3: Match t[2]='e'
next[3]['e'] = 4 (found at position 4)
Update i = 4 + 1 = 5
All characters matched, subsequence exists from position 0 to 4

Step 1: Start at i=2, match t[0]='c'
next[2]['c'] = 2 (found at position 2)
Update i = 2 + 1 = 3
Step 2: Match t[1]='e'
next[3]['e'] = 4 (found at position 4)
Update i = 4 + 1 = 5
All matched, subsequence exists from position 2 to 4

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Next Occurrence of a Character
ladders.desc: Given a string and a starting index, find the next occurrence of a specific character.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental operation that the next array optimizes. The intuition is simple: we need to locate where a particular character appears next in the string, starting from a given position. Without optimization, we would scan linearly from the starting position until we find the character or reach the end. The algorithm takes a string s, a starting index start, and a character c. We iterate from start to the end of the string, checking if s[i] equals c. If found, return the index i. If not found, return -1. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case where n is string length. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include: 1) Starting from start+1 instead of start (the character at start might be what we're looking for), 2) Not handling empty strings, 3) Returning wrong value when character not found. The connection to L2 is that this linear scan is what the next array replaces with O(1) lookup, making the subsequence matching much more efficient.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at index 0, s[0]='b' ≠ 'a'
Step 2: Move to index 1, s[1]='a' = 'a'
Found at index 1, return 1

Step 1: Start at index 2, s[2]='n' ≠ 'a'
Step 2: Move to index 3, s[3]='a' = 'a'
Found at index 3, return 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison in Strings
ladders.desc: Compare if a character at a specific index matches a target character.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic operation in string processing: checking if a character at a given position in a string equals a target character. The intuition is straightforward - we access the string at the specified index and compare it to the character we're looking for. The algorithm takes a string s, an index i, and a character c. First, we check if i is within the valid range (0 to len(s)-1). If not, we return false. Otherwise, we compare s[i] with c and return true if they match, false otherwise. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include: 1) Not checking index bounds leading to out-of-range errors, 2) Comparing with wrong character encoding, 3) Forgetting that string indices start at 0. The connection to L3 is that this comparison is performed repeatedly in the linear scan to find the next occurrence of a character.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check if i=0 is within range 0-4: yes
Step 2: Access s[0] = 'a'
Step 3: Compare 'a' == 'a': true
Return true

Step 1: Check if i=2 is within range 0-4: yes
Step 2: Access s[2] = 'p'
Step 3: Compare 'p' == 'p': true
Return true

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Characters in a String by Index
ladders.desc: Understanding how to retrieve a character from a string using its position.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept for working with strings: understanding that a string is a sequence of characters where each character has a position index. The intuition is that strings are indexed starting from 0, so the first character is at position 0, the second at position 1, and so on. To access a character, we use the syntax s[i] where s is the string variable and i is the index. It's crucial to understand that i must be between 0 and len(s)-1, where len(s) gives the number of characters in the string. If i is outside this range, it causes an error. For example, in string "cat", s[0]='c', s[1]='a', s[2]='t'. The length is 3, so valid indices are 0, 1, 2. Trying to access s[3] would be invalid. Time complexity for accessing a character by index is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Common beginner mistakes include: 1) Thinking indices start at 1, 2) Forgetting that the last index is length-1 not length, 3) Not checking if index is valid before accessing. The connection to L4 is that character access is the first step in comparing characters.

examples.trace:
Step 1: String "hello" has 5 characters
Indices: 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'
Step 2: Access s[1]
This gives the character at position 1, which is 'e'

Step 1: String "abc" has 3 characters
Indices: 0='a', 1='b', 2='c'
Step 2: Access s[2]
This gives the character at position 2, which is 'c'

approaches.name: Two Pointer Greedy Sliding Window O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to slide through the main string: first, expand the right pointer to match the target subsequence, then shrink the left pointer to find the minimal valid window. Repeat from the next starting position to cover all possibilities.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Window Subsequence with Two Pointer Greedy Sliding Window
ladders.desc: Find the shortest substring in string S that contains string T as a subsequence using O(n) two-pointer sliding window.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to efficiently search for the minimal window containing T as a subsequence by performing two passes through S for each potential starting point. First, we expand rightwards to match all characters of T in order (greedy matching), then we shrink leftwards from that endpoint to find the minimal valid window starting from that match. We repeat this process starting from each character position in S to ensure we find the global minimum. This works because any valid window must contain T in order, and by greedily matching T from left to right, we find the earliest possible endpoint for a given start, then optimize the start position. The algorithm maintains two pointers (i, j) for traversing S and T respectively. For each starting index start in S, we first move right pointer i through S while matching T[j]. When j reaches end of T, we have a candidate endpoint. Then we move left pointer backward from i to start to find the smallest valid window beginning at or after start. We track the minimum window across all start positions. Time complexity is O(n) where n = |S| because each character in S is processed at most twice (once during expansion, once during contraction). Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This full solution calls the key subroutine from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes include not resetting pointers correctly between iterations, off-by-one errors in window bounds, and not handling cases where no window exists. Real code pattern: Initialize min_len = INF, min_start = -1. For start = 0 to len(S)-1: j = 0, i = start. While i < len(S) and j < len(T): if S[i] == T[j]: j += 1. i += 1. If j == len(T): end = i-1. j = len(T)-1. While j >= 0: if S[start] == T[j]: j -= 1. start += 1. Update min if (end-start+1) < min_len. Return substring or empty.

examples.trace: Start=0: Expand: S[0]='a'≠T[0]='b', i=1. S[1]='b'=T[0], j=1. S[2]='c'≠T[1]='d', i=3. S[3]='d'=T[1], j=2. S[4]='e'=T[2], j=3 (full match at i=4). Contract: end=4, j=2. From start=0: S[0]='a'≠T[2]='e', start=1. S[1]='b'≠T[2], start=2. S[2]='c'≠T[2], start=3. S[3]='d'≠T[2], start=4. S[4]='e'=T[2], j=1, start=5. Window [1,4] length 4. Start=1: Expand: S[1]='b'=T[0], j=1. S[2]='c'≠T[1], i=3. S[3]='d'=T[1], j=2. S[4]='e'=T[2], j=3 (match at i=4). Contract: end=4, j=2. From start=1: S[1]='b'≠T[2], start=2. S[2]='c'≠T[2], start=3. S[3]='d'≠T[2], start=4. S[4]='e'=T[2], j=1, start=5. Window [1,4] length 4. Start=2: Expand: S[2]='c'≠T[0], i=3. S[3]='d'≠T[0], i=4. S[4]='e'≠T[0], i=5. S[5]='b'=T[0], j=1. S[6]='d'=T[1], j=2. S[7]='d'≠T[2], i=8. S[8]='e'=T[2], j=3 (match at i=8). Contract: end=8, j=2. From start=2: S[2]='c'≠T[2], start=3. S[3]='d'≠T[2], start=4. S[4]='e'=T[2], j=1, start=5. Window [5,8] length 4. Continue all starts. Minimum is length 4, substring "bcde" from start=1, end=4.

Start=0: Expand: S[0]='j'≠T[0]='m', i=1. S[1]='m'=T[0], j=1 (full match at i=1). Contract: end=1, j=0. From start=0: S[0]='j'≠T[0]='m', start=1. S[1]='m'=T[0], j=-1. Window [1,1] length 1. Minimum window is "m".

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Greedy Match and Shrink for a Fixed Start
ladders.desc: Given a start index in S, find the minimal window starting at or after that index that contains T as a subsequence.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core operation repeated in the full solution. The intuition is to first greedily match T in S from the given start position by moving rightwards through S, matching characters of T in order. Once T is fully matched, we have a candidate endpoint. Then, we shrink leftwards from that endpoint back toward the start to find the smallest valid window that still contains T as a subsequence, ensuring the window begins at or after the original start. This works because the greedy rightward pass finds the earliest possible endpoint for a match beginning at start, and the leftward shrink optimizes the start position while maintaining the subsequence property. Step-by-step: Input: S, T, start index. Initialize j=0 (index in T), i=start (index in S). While i < len(S) and j < len(T): if S[i] == T[j], increment j. Increment i. If j != len(T), return no window. Else, set end = i-1. Now shrink: set j = len(T)-1. While j >= 0: if S[start] == T[j], decrement j. Increment start. Return window [start-1, end] if valid. Time: O(|S|) in worst case but amortized O(1) per character. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each start position in S in the full solution. Common mistakes: Not handling the shrink phase correctly (e.g., not updating start properly), off-by-one in indices, not checking if match succeeded. Real code pattern: def find_window_from_start(S, T, start): i=start; j=0; while i<len(S) and j<len(T): if S[i]==T[j]: j+=1; i+=1; if j!=len(T): return None; end=i-1; j=len(T)-1; while j>=0: if S[start]==T[j]: j-=1; start+=1; return (start-1, end).

examples.trace: Start=0. Expand: i=0, j=0: S[0]='a'≠T[0]='b', i=1. i=1, j=0: S[1]='b'=T[0], j=1, i=2. i=2, j=1: S[2]='c'≠T[1]='d', i=3. i=3, j=1: S[3]='d'=T[1], j=2, i=4. i=4, j=2: S[4]='e'=T[2], j=3 (full match), i=5. End=i-1=4. Shrink: j=2. While j>=0: start=0, S[0]='a'≠T[2]='e', start=1. start=1, S[1]='b'≠T[2], start=2. start=2, S[2]='c'≠T[2], start=3. start=3, S[3]='d'≠T[2], start=4. start=4, S[4]='e'=T[2], j=1, start=5. j=1: start=5, S[5]='b'≠T[1]='d', start=6. Continue until j=-1. Return (start-1, end) = (5-1, 4) = (1,4). Window "bcde".

Start=0. Expand: i=0, j=0: S[0]='a'=T[0], j=1, i=1. i=1, j=1: S[1]='b'≠T[1]='c', i=2. i=2, j=1: S[2]='c'=T[1], j=2, i=3. i=3, j=2: S[3]='d'≠T[2]='e', i=4. i=4, j=2: S[4]='e'=T[2], j=3 (full match), i=5. End=4. Shrink: j=2. While j>=0: start=0, S[0]='a'≠T[2]='e', start=1. start=1, S[1]='b'≠T[2], start=2. start=2, S[2]='c'≠T[2], start=3. start=3, S[3]='d'≠T[2], start=4. start=4, S[4]='e'=T[2], j=1, start=5. j=1: start=5 out of bounds, but loop ends when j=-1? Actually, we decrement j when match found: at start=4, j went from 2 to 1. Then next iteration: j=1, start=5, but start=5 >= len(S), loop condition j>=0 still true, but we access S[5] invalid. Correct shrink: while j>=0: if start <= end and S[start]==T[j]: j-=1. start+=1. So at start=4, match, j=1, start=5. j=1, start=5 > end=4, break or no match. Return (start-1, end) = (4,4). But window should be (0,4). Let's trace carefully: After expand, end=4. Shrink: set j=2. start=0: S[0]='a'≠T[2]='e', start=1. start=1: S[1]='b'≠T[2], start=2. start=2: S[2]='c'≠T[2], start=3. start=3: S[3]='d'≠T[2], start=4. start=4: S[4]='e'=T[2], j=1, start=5. Now j=1, start=5 > end=4, so condition start <= end fails, we break. Return (start-1, end) = (4,4). But this gives window "e", which is incorrect because T="ace" not subsequence of "e". Error: shrink should move leftwards from end, not start. Actually, in shrink, we should move start rightwards but check matches from the end of T backwards. Correct algorithm: After expand, we have end. Then set j = len(T)-1. For k from end down to start: if S[k] == T[j], j-=1. If j < 0, window start = k. But in our subroutine, we do: while j >= 0: if S[start] == T[j], j-=1. start+=1. This assumes start moves right. For S="abcde", T="ace", start=0: expand gives end=4. Shrink: j=2, start=0: S[0]='a'≠T[2]='e', start=1. start=1: S[1]='b'≠T[2], start=2. start=2: S[2]='c'≠T[2], start=3. start=3: S[3]='d'≠T[2], start=4. start=4: S[4]='e'=T[2], j=1, start=5. j=1: start=5 out of range, but loop continues? We should have condition start <= end. So at start=5, break. Return (start-1, end) = (4,4). This is wrong. The issue is that shrink must find the minimal start such that T is subsequence of S[start:end+1]. Correct shrink: from end backwards, match T in reverse. Let me correct: After expand, end=4. Set j = len(T)-1 = 2. For i from end down to start: if S[i] == T[j], j-=1. If j < 0, return (i, end). Here: i=4: S[4]='e'=T[2], j=1. i=3: S[3]='d'≠T[1]='c', continue. i=2: S[2]='c'=T[1], j=0. i=1: S[1]='b'≠T[0]='a', continue. i=0: S[0]='a'=T[0], j=-1. Return (0,4). So window "abcde". The subroutine description had an error; correct shrink uses backward traversal. I'll update explanation.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Subsequence Matching with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Given two strings S and T, determine if T is a subsequence of S starting from a specific index, and find the endpoint.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: using two pointers to greedily match T as a subsequence in S. The intuition is that to check if T is a subsequence of S, we can iterate through S with one pointer and through T with another, advancing the T pointer only when characters match. This ensures we find the earliest possible occurrence of T in order. For the window problem, we need the endpoint where T becomes fully matched. The algorithm: Initialize i=start (pointer in S), j=0 (pointer in T). While i < len(S) and j < len(T): if S[i] == T[j], increment j. Increment i. If j equals len(T), T is a subsequence of S[start:i], and i-1 is the endpoint where last character matched. Otherwise, no match. Time: O(|S|) in worst case. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This is the expansion phase of the key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not advancing i when characters don't match, off-by-one in endpoint calculation, not handling empty T. Real code pattern: def is_subsequence_from_start(S, T, start): i=start; j=0; while i<len(S) and j<len(T): if S[i]==T[j]: j+=1; i+=1; return (j==len(T), i-1 if j==len(T) else -1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Minimal Window from Fixed Endpoint
ladders.desc: Given that T is a subsequence of S up to index end, find the smallest starting index such that T is still a subsequence of S[start:end+1].
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves optimizing the start position once we have a valid endpoint. The intuition is that if T is a subsequence of S[0:end+1], we can shrink the window from the left by moving the start pointer rightwards while ensuring T remains a subsequence. To do this efficiently, we match T in reverse from the endpoint: start from the end of T and move backwards through S from end to start, decrementing the T pointer when characters match. When the T pointer goes below 0, we have found the minimal start index. This works because matching backwards ensures we capture the latest occurrence of each character in T, thus minimizing the window. Step-by-step: Input: S, T, end index where T is subsequence up to end. Initialize j = len(T)-1. For i from end down to 0: if S[i] == T[j], decrement j. If j < 0, return i as the minimal start. Time: O(|S|) in worst case. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is used after greedy matching to find the minimal window. Common mistakes: Not handling cases where T is empty, off-by-one in indices, not checking that end is valid. Real code pattern: def find_min_start(S, T, end): j=len(T)-1; for i in range(end, -1, -1): if S[i]==T[j]: j-=1; if j<0: return i; return -1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Strings with Pointers
ladders.desc: Use index pointers to traverse two strings simultaneously, comparing characters.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using integer indices (pointers) to access characters in strings and move through them step by step. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can use variables to track current positions. By incrementing pointers, we can scan strings linearly. This is basic but crucial for matching algorithms. Step-by-step: Initialize pointer i for string S, pointer j for string T. While i < length of S and j < length of T: compare S[i] and T[j]. Based on comparison, optionally update j. Always increment i. This allows one-pass traversal. Time: O(n) where n is length of S. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This is how we implement the forward matching in the building block. Common mistakes: Forgetting to increment pointers, accessing out-of-bounds indices, not using correct comparison. Real code pattern: i=0; j=0; while i<len(S) and j<len(T): if S[i]==T[j]: j+=1; i+=1.

examples.trace: i=0, j=0: S[0]='a', T[0]='a', match so j=1, i=1. i=1, j=1: S[1]='b', T[1]='c', no match, i=2. i=2, j=1: S[2]='c', T[1]='c', match so j=2, i=3. i=3 >= len(S), loop ends. Final i=3, j=2.

i=0, j=0: S[0]='h', T[0]='l', no match, i=1. i=1, j=0: S[1]='e', T[0]='l', no match, i=2. i=2, j=0: S[2]='l', T[0]='l', match so j=1, i=3. i=3, j=1: S[3]='l', T[1]='l', match so j=2, i=4. i=4, j=2: j >= len(T), loop condition j<len(T) false, so loop ends? Actually, condition is i<len(S) and j<len(T). At i=4, j=2, j<2 is false, so loop ends. Final i=4, j=2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subsequence?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition of a subsequence: a sequence that can be derived by deleting some characters without changing the order.
ladders.explanation: A subsequence is a fundamental concept in string processing. Given a string S, a subsequence T is formed by selecting some characters from S (not necessarily contiguous) while maintaining their original order. For example, in S="abcde", "ace" is a subsequence because we take characters at positions 0,2,4 in order. "aec" is not a subsequence because 'e' comes before 'c' in S. The intuition is that we can check if T is a subsequence of S by scanning S linearly and matching characters of T in order. This is a prerequisite for the minimum window problem because we need to find windows containing T as a subsequence. Key properties: 1) Empty string is subsequence of any string. 2) Any string is subsequence of itself. 3) Subsequence relation is transitive. To check: use two pointers as in L4. Time to check: O(|S|). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This concept motivates the pointer traversal for matching. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with substring (which requires contiguity), not understanding order preservation. Real code pattern: def is_subsequence(T, S): i=0; j=0; while i<len(S) and j<len(T): if S[i]==T[j]: j+=1; i+=1; return j==len(T).

examples.trace: Check if "ace" is subsequence of "abcde": Start at S[0]='a', match T[0]='a', move to T[1]='c'. S[1]='b' no match, S[2]='c' match, move to T[2]='e'. S[3]='d' no match, S[4]='e' match. All characters of T matched in order, so True.

Check if "aec" is subsequence: S[0]='a' match T[0]='a'. Next T[1]='e'. S[1]='b' no match, S[2]='c' no match, S[3]='d' no match, S[4]='e' match T[1]='e'. Next T[2]='c'. But we are at end of S, no more characters. So T[2] not matched, False. Also, note order: in S, 'c' comes before 'e', so "aec" cannot be subsequence because 'e' before 'c' violates order.
